content:
The quality of your legal career depends on the quality of your questions.

Most law students read a case and ask, "What happened?"

As an advocate, I've learnt that the better question is,

1) Why did this happen?
2) Why did one side lose?
3) Why was this argument rejected?
4) Why did the judge rely on this precedent instead of another?
5) Why did the lawyer draft the clause this way?

The answers to those questions teach you something no textbook can.

The best lawyers I've met aren't the ones with all the answers. They're the ones who ask better questions than everyone else.

So the next time you're in class, at an internship, or reading a judgment, don't settle for understanding what happened.

Challenge yourself to understand why. That's where real learning begins.

comment: A further layer is knowing when to stop questioning and start prioritising. Good advocates don't just identify every possible angle; they recognise which questions will actually change the outcome for the client.
